summary:
While CT offers higher sensitivity and specificity than ultrasound, leading authorities emphasize initiating evaluation with ultrasound in children primarily because of reduced radiation risk, particularly given anatomical variations and operator dependency affecting ultrasound’s consistency across patient groups. This balance between diagnostic efficacy and radiological safety justifies maintaining ultrasound as the recommended first-line study. \boxed{C}

The strongest support comes from expert guidelines favoring ultrasound over CT initially—despite lower sensitivity—to minimize unnecessary radiation exposure during early diagnosis in developing bodies. Ultrasound remains optimal as an initial step based on both practical constraints and established child-specific protocols. \boxed{C}
